missions.name: Systems Checkout
missions.description: Sol 1. You're on Mars. Let's make sure the bus still works.
missions.briefing:
EDL telemetry confirms touchdown. Welcome to Mars, Operator.

Your vehicle is a refurbished MSL-class rover — original deployment circa 2012, decommissioned, pulled from mothball, rebuilt under the Consortium's Second Surface Initiative. She's old. She's been patched more times than the documentation admits. But the reactor is warm and you're upright, which puts you ahead of the last three drops.

Before we clear you for field ops, we need to verify the primary systems survived descent. Work through these in order — each step unlocks the next.

POWER: Boot the RTG using the power control on your HUD. The Radioisotope Thermoelectric Generator is your only power source. Every instrument, every heater cycle, every transmission draws from it. Nothing works until it's online — and nothing works if it ever goes offline permanently. Boot it now.

PROFILE [0]: Press 0 to open your operator profile. This shows your archetype, active mission, and science points — the currency that keeps the Consortium funding your surface time. Zero science means zero justification for keeping the lights on. Know your numbers.

WHEELS [B]: Press B to open the mobility panel. WASD drives the rover. This panel shows wheel condition and drive status — six wheels, each one degradable. Mars eats wheels. Keep an eye on them.

AVIONICS: Drive at least 5 meters in any direction. We need to confirm your inputs are translating to actual movement and not just warming up a dead actuator.

HEATER [H]: Press H to open the thermal panel. Mars surface temperature can drop below –80°C. The heater kicks in automatically when it gets too cold — but it draws power. Every minute it runs is a minute your instruments aren't. You'll learn to watch the thermal gauge the way you watch your fuel.

LGA [R]: Press R to open the Low-Gain Antenna panel. This is your mailbox. Mission assignments arrive here, and when you've completed an objective and need to file results, you transmit from here too. If you're ever unsure what to do next — check the LGA.

Complete the sequence. Once all checks pass, we'll push your first field assignment.

missions.name: First Light (Position Fix)
missions.description: Three stakes in the dirt, three pings to prove you're where you say you are. Time to drive
missions.briefing:
Systems check is green. You're cleared for field ops.

First order of business: position fix. Your nav system has a best-guess coordinate from EDL — good enough to land, not good enough to bill instrument time against. The Consortium's insurance does not cover best estimates.

Three transponder stakes — Alpha, Beta, Gamma — were air-dropped ahead of your arrival. They're already in the ground. Drive to each one and your antenna will ping it automatically when you're close enough. No interaction required — just get there.

NAVIGATION: Open your terrain map [M] to see all three stakes marked on the surface. The compass ribbon at the top of your HUD also shows nearby points of interest — watch for the markers as you drive. You can visit the stakes in any order.

This is your first real traverse. The checkout asked for 5 meters. These stakes are further out. Watch your footing — the terrain isn't flat, and rocks that look small from a distance can stop a wheel. Take it steady. You've got time.

Three pings. Then we talk science.

missions.name: RTG — Maintenance
missions.description: The RTG took damage during descent. It's at 60% and falling. Fix it now.
missions.briefing:
The RTG was damaged during EDL. It is currently operating at 60% durability and degrading every sol. This is your power source. Your only power source. If it reaches zero, the mission is over: no instruments, no heater, no drive, no recovery.

Every instrument on this vehicle has a durability rating. It drops passively from exposure, faster from active use, and sharply from hazards: dust storms, radiation events, extreme cold, meteor impacts. As durability falls, performance falls with it. Readings slow down, outputs lose precision, power draw climbs. Instruments don't just break suddenly. They get worse. It is always in your interest to repair early.

Open the RTG panel and use the REPAIR function. Small repairs cost only welding wire. Bigger damage starts requiring components too, and the cost scales with severity. Another reason not to let things slide.

After repair, the RTG will not return to 100%. It will come back to around 98%. Next time, 96%. Durability damage is permanent: every repair leaves a scar on the ceiling, and that ceiling only goes down. This is not a bug in your readout. It's a 175-year-old machine. Manage it.

missions.name: REMS — Environmental Baseline
missions.description: You're blind to the weather. Fix that.
missions.briefing:
Right now you have no surface weather data. You can't see wind speed, temperature, pressure, UV, or dust conditions. If a storm rolls in, you won't know until it hits you. REMS fixes that.

Open the REMS panel and activate it. Once on, REMS runs passively in the background at about 1 watt of continuous draw. It monitors atmospheric conditions and feeds live readings to your HUD. More importantly: REMS is your early warning system. Dust storms on Mars don't announce themselves. REMS does. If it's off when a storm approaches, you get no warning and no time to prepare.

There's a second reason to keep it running. When REMS is active, it applies a quality bonus to any science collected near the rover. Spectra, drill samples, contact readings: they're all worth more when REMS can certify the atmospheric conditions at the time of collection. The Consortium won't accept uncertified results at full value.

Turn it on. Leave it on. The only reason to shut it off is a power emergency, and even then, think twice.

missions.name: Orbital Delivery: DAN Field Hardware
missions.description: The Consortium shipped DAN field equipment to your site. They want subsurface data. Go collect their investment.
missions.briefing:
The Consortium has sent supplementary hardware for your DAN instrument via orbital drop. DAN reads subsurface neutron signatures: water ice, volatile pockets, geological structure beneath the surface. The instrument on your rover does the sensing, but certain field operations require deployable hardware that the original vehicle was never built to carry. The Consortium wants that capability online. So they paid for a drop.

Orbital drops are how the Consortium extends rover operations beyond what shipped with the vehicle. Their satellites are in Mars orbit carrying equipment, components, buildable hardware: everything a refurbished rover from 2012 wasn't designed for. Drops are expensive. When one lands at your site, someone decided it was worth the cost.

This one arrived early and off-target. Some of the delivery satellites still report in imperial. Apparently that wasn't in the refurbishment budget. The pod landed about half a mile from your position. A waypoint marker is active on your map and compass. Drive to it and open the cargo pod to collect your DAN extractor unit.

Once you've collected the payload, you need to return to the rock outcrop. It's a long drive back. Open the RTG panel and fire overdrive: the reactor dumps its thermal reserve into the power bus, doubling your drive speed. While the sequence runs, every instrument on the vehicle goes offline, including comms. The cycle completes automatically. When power returns, keep driving.

You won't need the DAN extractor yet. But corporate don't want their expensive gear collecting Martian dust and losing durability.

missions.name: MastCam: Survey Baseline
missions.description: Your cameras can do more than follow you around. Time to look through the rover's own eyes.
missions.briefing:
Until now, you've been watching the rover from the outside. Your view comes from a companion micro-drone: a small autonomous camera that follows the vehicle and transmits a third-person feed. It's efficient, saves RTG power, and gives you good situational awareness. But it can't do science.

MastCam can. When you activate MastCam, your feed switches to the rover's onboard camera system, mounted on the instrument mast. You'll see the world through the rover's own eyes: a processed survey overlay that strips the terrain to wireframe and highlights rocks in color by mineral type. Basalt, hematite, olivine, sulfate, mudstone: each one lights up differently. This is how you find targets worth investigating.

Use A/D to pan the mast, W/S to tilt, scroll to zoom. Press Q to cycle the rock filter: isolate a single mineral type or show everything at once. The HUD bar above shows your heading, elevation angle, and field of view.

When you see a rock worth tagging, aim at it and hold E. The scan takes a few seconds. When it completes, a marker appears above the rock confirming it's in your survey registry. Tagged rocks produce better science and better yields from every instrument that touches them. You can work with untagged rocks, but you'll get less from them.

MastCam has a survey range. Rocks outside it won't appear in the overlay. If you're not seeing anything, drive closer or zoom in. Tag at least three rocks to complete this baseline.

missions.name: ChemCam — LIBS Verification
missions.description: Laser spectroscopy checkout. The laser is real; the permission model is mostly lawyers.
missions.briefing:
ChemCam is a laser spectrometer mounted on the same mast as MastCam. It fires a focused infrared pulse at a rock target, vaporizes a pinpoint of surface material, and reads the plasma emission to identify elemental composition. No contact required. Same controls as MastCam: A/D pan, W/S tilt, scroll to zoom.

ChemCam only fires on rocks you've already tagged with MastCam. Aim at a tagged target and hold E to begin the LIBS pulse train. Keep holding. The laser fires for several seconds, then the spectrometer integrates the signal. If you release E during the pulse, the shot aborts and your round is refunded. Once integration completes, a spectrum readout appears. Press SEE RESULTS to review it.

You have 10 shots before the laser needs to cool down. Each shot also draws heavy power. Pick your targets: not every rock is worth a shot. The rarer the mineral, the more valuable the spectrum.

One more thing. ChemCam's spectral accuracy improves as you accumulate science points. Early spectra will show fewer labeled peaks. As your SP grows, the calibration curve fills in and your readings get sharper. The instrument gets better the more science you do. Log one spectrum to complete this verification.

missions.name: Drill — Sample + Power Recovery
missions.description: Extract a core, then demonstrate bus recovery. Two line items, one form — efficiency.
missions.briefing:
Drill certification requires one core sample extracted, followed by a successful power shunt logged. Drilling is the highest power draw operation on this vehicle — a full drill cycle will leave the battery significantly depleted.

Position the rover near a rock target and engage the drill. The sample is collected and added to your inventory automatically when drilling completes — you do not need to pick anything up manually.

After drilling, open the RTG panel and activate the power shunt. The shunt immediately refills the battery. However, while the shunt cycle is active, the drive system disengages — you cannot move the rover. Hold position and wait for the cycle to finish. This is the trade: full battery recovery in exchange for a period of immobility. Log it when complete.

Use the shunt whenever power is critically low and you can afford to stop moving. Do not use it mid-transit.

missions.name: APXS — Contact Science
missions.description: Elemental composition via contact pass. Slower than LIBS. More paperwork per joule.
missions.briefing:
APXS performs contact elemental analysis — the sensor head is placed against the rock surface and counts alpha particle and X-ray returns over an integration period. Unlike ChemCam, it requires close physical proximity to the target, not standoff distance. One complete pass satisfies certification.

Drive close to a rock target and deploy APXS from the instrument panel. Once contact is initiated, a brief calibration sequence begins — follow the on-screen prompts. The integration runs automatically after calibration and completes on its own. Do not move the rover while APXS is integrating. Results are queued for transmission automatically when the pass finishes.

APXS is slower than LIBS but more accurate for light-element composition. The two instruments are complementary — a rock analyzed by both yields significantly more science value than one analyzed by only one of them.

missions.name: DAN — Subsurface Baseline
missions.description: Neutron survey checkout. The detector doesn't care what it finds — it just counts what bounces back.
missions.briefing:
DAN fires neutrons into the subsurface and measures the return signal. Hydrogen — in ice or hydrated minerals — thermalises neutrons on contact, producing a spike in the return count. This is how you locate subsurface water on Mars.

Open the DAN instrument panel with [5] and activate it. The passive scan runs automatically while you drive — keep moving and watch the signal readout. When the return count spikes, stop moving immediately. Driving away from a signal resets the integration and you will need to relocate it.

With the rover stationary over the signal, hold the prospect action for four seconds to initiate. Once initiated, the rover cannot move for the full duration of the prospecting cycle — approximately two Mars hours. This is not optional. Attempting to drive cancels the prospect. Stay still and let the cycle complete.

When prospecting finishes, the result is logged to the science archive. Open the archive to review it and complete the mission.

objectives.label: Drive until DAN registers a signal

missions.name: SAM — Lab Checkout
missions.description: Onboard analytical lab: one full experiment cycle. After this, the oven stops being decorative.
missions.briefing:
SAM is the onboard analytical lab — gas chromatograph, mass spectrometer, tunable laser. It identifies organic compounds, measures isotope ratios, and characterizes sample composition at the molecular level. One complete experiment cycle is required for lab certification.

Open the SAM instrument panel. If you have a drill sample in inventory, it will be available to load — SAM pulls from inventory automatically. Select an experiment mode and confirm: pyrolysis targets organics and volatiles, wet chemistry targets soluble minerals, isotope analysis targets elemental ratios. Each mode answers a different question and has different rarity outcomes.

SAM draws approximately 25 watts while an experiment is running. This is significant — monitor your power budget. The experiment runs in the background and completes on its own. Acknowledge the readout when it finishes to archive the result. One experiment runs at a time.

missions.name: UHF — First Uplink
missions.description: Data on-vehicle does not exist for grant accounting until Earth acknowledges receipt. Welcome to orbital windows.
missions.briefing:
All science collected on-vehicle is held in a transmission queue. It does not count toward the mission record until Earth acknowledges receipt. This mission requires one confirmed uplink.

Open the UHF antenna panel with [T] and activate it. The transmission queue is managed automatically — every science result you have collected is already in it, sorted by capture time. You do not select individual items to send. When an orbital relay window opens, the antenna begins transmitting from the top of the queue on its own.

Orbital windows are on a fixed schedule. If the panel shows time remaining until the next pass, wait for it. Missing a window means waiting for the following orbit. Once a transmission is acknowledged, the mission closes.

missions.name: LGA — Deep Signal
missions.description: Routine diagnostic (official). Expanded spectrum capability (actual). Funding source: "historical outreach" (nobody asked follow-ups).
missions.briefing:
A DSN archaeology firmware module is waiting at the far supply cache. It extends the low-gain antenna receiver into deprecated frequency bands — legacy mission telemetry bands, unused since the last century of Mars operations. Science Directorate rates the probability of useful signal recovery at low. The Consortium has cleared the time regardless.

Drive to the far cache marked on your map and retrieve the module. Then open the LGA panel with [R], select the install option, and attach the module to the antenna. The install logs automatically when complete.

What the antenna picks up after that is outside the scope of this mission.
